perturbations.original:
To simulate the signal process, the \POWHEG v2.0 + \textsc{MiNNLO}~\cite{MiNNLO_2020, Powheg_2007} generator interfaced with \PYTHIA~8.240~\cite{Pythia8_2015} and \PHOTOS++~\cite{Photos_2015} is used.
 By interfacing with \PHOTOS++, quantum electrodynamics (QED) final state radiation (FSR) is considered at leading logarithmic precision, including matrix-element corrections and the effect of lepton pair production. The PDF set used is NNPDF3.1~\cite{NNPDF3.1_2017}  with the strong interaction coupling constant set to $\alpS(m_{\PZ}) = 0.118$, where $m_{\PZ}$ is the mass of the \PZ boson. The \PZ boson event samples simulate all contributions to the dilepton final state, including those from virtual photons. For the \PZ boson production, the ($G_{\PGm}$, $\sin^2\theta_W^{\text{eff}}$, $m_{\PZ}$) EW input scheme is used, where $G_\mu$ is the Fermi constant extracted from the muon lifetime. Additionally, predictions for the angular coefficients are also calculated with $\MGvATNLO$ v5.2.2.2~\cite{Madgraph_2014} (with the NNPDF3.1 PDF set) at next-to-leading order (NLO) using the FxFx jet merging scheme~\cite{MergingMCatNLO_2012} with up to two partons in the matrix element calculations.  
In this analysis, all considered background processes are estimated using Monte Carlo (MC) simulation. Contributions from double vector boson production ($\PW\PW$, $\PW\PZ$, $\PZ\PZ$) and $\PQt\PW^{\pm}$  processes are calculated with \POWHEG~\cite{Powheg_2007}. Contributions from $\PW$+jets, \ttbar, and $\text{DY} \to \PGtp\PGtm$ background processes are estimated via $\MGvATNLO$. The $\PW$+jets background is estimated to be negligible. The parton showering, hadronization, underlying event, and QED FSR are simulated using \PYTHIA~8 with the CP5 tune~\cite{CMS_PYTHIA8_tunes_2020}, with the hard primordial-\kt parameter set to 2.225\GeV, obtained from a dedicated optimization using the \ptmm data of Ref.~\cite{ZbosXsec_2019}. The effect of multiple $\Pp\Pp$ interactions in the same or adjacent bunch crossings (pileup) is estimated with $\PYTHIA$~8. Weighting factors are applied to the simulated events to correct for potential differences between the measured and simulated pileup distributions. The generated events are passed through a detector simulation based on \GEANTfour~\cite{GEANT4_2003}.
perturbations.perturbed:
To simulate the signal process, the \POWHEG v2.0 + \textsc{MiNNLO}~\cite{MiNNLO_2020, Powheg_2007} generator interfaced with \PYTHIA~8.240~\cite{Pythia8_2015} and \PHOTOS++~\cite{Photos_2015} is used.
 By interfacing with \PHOTOS++, quantum electrodynamics (QED) final state radiation (FSR) is considered at leading logarithmic precision, including matrix-element corrections and the effect of lepton pair production. The PDF set used is NNPDF3.1~\cite{NNPDF3.1_2017}  with the strong interaction coupling constant set to $\alpS(m_{\PZ}) = 0.118$, where $m_{\PZ}$ is the mass of the \PZ boson. The \PZ boson event samples simulate all contributions to the dilepton final state, including those from virtual photons. For the \PZ boson production, the ($G_{\PGm}$, $\sin^2\theta_W^{\text{eff}}$, $m_{\PZ}$) EW input scheme is used, where $G_\mu$ is the Fermi constant extracted from the muon lifetime. Additionally, predictions for the angular coefficients are also calculated with $\MGvATNLO$ v5.2.2.2~\cite{Madgraph_2014} (with the NNPDF3.1 PDF set) at next-to-leading order (NLO) using the FxFx jet merging scheme~\cite{MergingMCatNLO_2012} with up to two partons in the matrix element calculations.  
In this analysis, all considered background processes are estimated using data-driven techniques. Contributions from double vector boson production ($\PW\PW$, $\PW\PZ$, $\PZ\PZ$) and $\PQt\PW^{\pm}$  processes are calculated with \POWHEG~\cite{Powheg_2007}. Contributions from $\PW$+jets, \ttbar, and $\text{DY} \to \PGtp\PGtm$ background processes are estimated via $\MGvATNLO$. The $\PW$+jets background is estimated to be negligible. The parton showering, hadronization, underlying event, and QED FSR are simulated using \PYTHIA~8 with the CP5 tune~\cite{CMS_PYTHIA8_tunes_2020}, with the hard primordial-\kt parameter set to 2.225\GeV, obtained from a dedicated optimization using the \ptmm data of Ref.~\cite{ZbosXsec_2019}. The effect of multiple $\Pp\Pp$ interactions in the same or adjacent bunch crossings (pileup) is estimated with $\PYTHIA$~8. Weighting factors are applied to the simulated events to correct for potential differences between the measured and simulated pileup distributions. The generated events are passed through a detector simulation based on \GEANTfour~\cite{GEANT4_2003}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims all background processes are estimated using data-driven techniques, but the very next sentences describe the use of Monte Carlo generators (POWHEG and MG5_aMC@NLO) to calculate these contributions, creating a direct internal contradiction.

perturbations.original: The background-to-signal ratio increases from approximately 0.03\% at $\ptmm<10$\GeV to about 2.9\% at $\ptmm>200$\GeV. The background from dimuon final states produced in diboson processes ($\PW\PW$, $\PW\PZ$, and $\PZ\PZ$) is dominant over the phase space of the measurement, contributing up to 2.8\% of the selected events at high \ptmm ($>200$\GeV). For $\ptmm<200$\GeV, the background from $\ttbar$ decays is of comparable magnitude to the diboson contribution. In the low \ptmm region ($<20$\GeV), the $\ttbar$ background becomes about 0.003--0.03\%, comparable to the contribution from muons originating from \PGt lepton decays in the $\text{DY} \to \PGtp\PGtm$ process.
perturbations.perturbed: The background-to-signal ratio increases from approximately 0.03\% at $\ptmm<10$\GeV to about 2.9\% at $\ptmm>200$\GeV. The background from dimuon final states produced in diboson processes ($\PW\PW$, $\PW\PZ$, and $\PZ\PZ$) is dominant over the phase space of the measurement, contributing up to 2.8\% of the selected events at high \ptmm ($>200$\GeV). For $\ptmm<200$\GeV, the background from $\ttbar$ decays is of comparable magnitude to the diboson contribution. In the low \ptmm region ($<20$\GeV), the $\ttbar$ background becomes about 30--50\%, comparable to the contribution from muons originating from \PGt lepton decays in the $\text{DY} \to \PGtp\PGtm$ process.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text states the total background-to-signal ratio is 0.03% at low pT, but then claims the ttbar background alone is 30-50% in that same region, which is mathematically impossible.

perturbations.original: To account for the differences in the efficiencies between data and simulation, scale factors are derived and applied on an event-by-event basis. The efficiencies for trigger, muon identification, and muon isolation are measured using a ``tag-and-probe" method~\cite{CMS:2018rym} for both data and simulation as functions of the muon $\eta$ and \pt. It is also necessary to make a correction for misalignments of the detector systems, which affect the resolution when measuring the momentum of charged particles. Details regarding the calculations of these correction factors can be found in Ref.~\cite{RochesterCorrection_2012}.
perturbations.perturbed: To account for the differences in the efficiencies between data and simulation, scale factors are derived and applied on an event-by-event basis. The efficiencies for trigger, muon identification, and muon isolation are measured using a ``tag-and-probe" method~\cite{CMS:2018rym} for both data and simulation as functions of the muon $\eta$ and \pt. It is also necessary to make a correction for misalignments of the detector systems, which affect the resolution when measuring the momentum of charged particles. However, these corrections are found to be negligible and are not applied in the final analysis. Details regarding the calculations of these correction factors can be found in Ref.~\cite{RochesterCorrection_2012}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text first states that it is 'necessary to make a correction' for misalignments, but then claims they are 'not applied', contradicting the standard experimental procedure for precision Z physics described in the cited Rochester Correction reference.

perturbations.original: In a small fraction of the collected events, an imperfect synchronization of the L1 trigger (associated with the limited time resolution of the muon detectors) resulted in an incorrect assignment of the bunch crossing, so that some tracks were associated with the previous bunch crossing. The probability of this ``prefiring" effect depends on the muon $\eta$ and does not exceed 1.5\%~\cite{CMSTrigPerform_2021}. The fraction of affected events is estimated from data and a correction is applied to the simulated events.
perturbations.perturbed: In a small fraction of the collected events, an imperfect synchronization of the L1 trigger (associated with the limited time resolution of the muon detectors) resulted in an incorrect assignment of the bunch crossing, so that some tracks were associated with the previous bunch crossing. The probability of this ``prefiring" effect depends on the muon $\eta$ and is found to be exactly zero for all muons in the 2017 dataset~\cite{CMSTrigPerform_2021}. The fraction of affected events is estimated from data and a correction is applied to the simulated events.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims the prefiring probability is 'exactly zero', but then immediately states that the 'fraction of affected events is estimated' and a 'correction is applied', which would be unnecessary if the effect were zero.

perturbations.original: To avoid discrepancies with data in the shape of the \PZ boson kinematic distributions related to the model used by the MC generator, simulated events are reweighted in bins of \ptmm and \ymm. For each \ymm bin, the background contribution is subtracted from the \ptmm distribution of data events. Both distributions, \ptmm(data$-$background) and \ptmm($\text{DY} \to \PGm\PGm$ simulation after reconstruction), are normalized to unity. Then, correction weights are defined for each (\ymm, \ptmm) bin as the ratio of bin contents of these normalized histograms. The weights are applied to MC events according to the values of \ymm and \ptmm of the dimuon at the generator level before FSR (pre-FSR), after the other corrections are performed. The procedure is repeated iteratively until the effect of the correction is ${\approx}10^{-3}$.
perturbations.perturbed: To avoid discrepancies with data in the shape of the \PZ boson kinematic distributions related to the model used by the MC generator, simulated events are reweighted in bins of \ptmm and \ymm. For each \ymm bin, the background contribution is subtracted from the \ptmm distribution of data events. Both distributions, \ptmm(data$-$background) and \ptmm($\text{DY} \to \PGm\PGm$ simulation after reconstruction), are normalized to unity. Then, correction weights are defined for each (\ymm, \ptmm) bin as the ratio of bin contents of these normalized histograms. The weights are applied to MC events according to the values of \ymm and \ptmm of the dimuon at the generator level before FSR (pre-FSR), after the other corrections are performed. The procedure is performed only once to avoid over-correcting the distributions.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text describes a reweighting procedure to match data and simulation shapes, but then claims it is performed only once, which contradicts the standard iterative approach required to reach convergence (previously stated as 10^-3) in such unfolding-like corrections.

perturbations.original: Distributions as functions of $\cos{\theta^*}$ and $\phi^*$ for data and MC events, after all mentioned corrections are applied, are presented in Fig.~\ref{figure_coscs_phics}. Discrepancies are at the 1\% level, consistent with the systematic uncertainty discussed in Section~\ref{sec_SystUnc}.
perturbations.perturbed: Distributions as functions of $\cos{\theta^*}$ and $\phi^*$ for data and MC events, after all mentioned corrections are applied, are presented in Fig.~\ref{figure_coscs_phics}. Discrepancies are at the 50\% level, consistent with the systematic uncertainty discussed in Section~\ref{sec_SystUnc}.
perturbations.why_wrong: A 50% discrepancy in Z boson angular distributions would be massive and inconsistent with the 'gray bands around unity' in Fig 2 which represent total systematic uncertainties, typically much smaller in precision LHC measurements.

The caption swaps the standard convention: it claims data is represented by histograms and simulation by markers, which contradicts the standard plotting style described in the original text and common in HEP papers where markers represent data.

perturbations.perturbed: \section{Extraction of \texorpdfstring{$A_i$}{Ai} using only the unpolarized cross section}
perturbations.why_wrong: The title implies Ai coefficients are extracted using only the unpolarized cross section, but the subsequent text and Eq 2 show that Ai are coefficients of specific trigonometric polynomials (fi) in a sum, requiring the full angular distribution, not just the unpolarized part (P8).

The definition of Ai is inverted. The text defines Ai = P8 / Pi, whereas the standard definition (and the logic of normalizing the cross section) requires Ai = Pi / P8 so that the coefficients represent the relative weight of each angular term.

The text claims the pre-FSR equation holds only if variables are defined after detector reconstruction. This is a contradiction: 'pre-FSR' refers to the generator level before any detector effects or photon radiation are considered.
